speaker: Guest
text_en: Undeclared wars are commonplace. Tragically, our government engages in preemptive war otherwise known as aggression with no complaints from the American people. Sadly, we have become accustomed to living with the illegitimate use of force by government. To develop a truly free society, the issue of initiating force must be understood and rejected.
text: 未宣而战早已司空见惯。可悲的是，我们的政府发动先发制人的战争，也就是所谓的侵略，而美国民众对此几乎毫无异议。令人遗憾的是，我们已经习惯了政府以不正当方式使用武力。要建立一个真正自由的社会，就必须理解并拒绝主动使用武力这个问题。

speaker: Guest
text_en: What if sometimes, to love your country, you had to alter or abolish the government? What if Jefferson was right? What if that government is best which governs least? What if it is dangerous to be right when the government is wrong? What if it is better to perish fighting for freedom than to live as a slave? What if freedom's greatest hour of danger is now?
text: 如果有时候，爱你的国家就意味着你必须改革甚至废除政府呢？如果 Jefferson 是对的呢？如果最好的政府就是管得最少的政府呢？如果当政府错了的时候，正确反而会变得危险呢？如果为自由而战、哪怕战死，也比像奴隶一样活着更好呢？如果自由最危险的时刻就是现在呢？

speaker: Host
text_en: Hi, everyone. Judge Andrew Napolitano here for Judging Freedom. Today is Tuesday, May 26, 2026. Professor John Mearsheimer will be with us in just a moment on the neocons and their lust for war. But first this.
text: 大家好，我是 Andrew Napolitano 法官，欢迎收看 Judging Freedom。今天是 2026 年 5 月 26 日，星期二。John Mearsheimer 教授马上就会来到节目，和我们谈谈 neocons 以及他们对战争的迷恋。不过先看这个。

speaker: Host
text_en: If the last few years have taught us anything, it's that the systems we rely on can fail. And when they do, we're on our own. That's why I want to tell you about My Patriot Supply, America's original patriot preparedness company.
text: 如果说过去这几年教会了我们什么，那就是我们所依赖的那些系统是会失灵的。而一旦失灵，我们就只能靠自己了。所以我想跟大家介绍一下 My Patriot Supply，美国最早的爱国者应急储备公司。

speaker: Host
text_en: When you get their flagship solar backup generator, the ultra-powerful Grid Doctor 3300, you'll also get over $1,000 worth of survival essentials absolutely free. Four weeks worth of emergency food, water filtration, and more, all absolutely free.
text: 如果你购买他们的旗舰太阳能备用发电机——超强大的 Grid Doctor 3300——你还会额外免费获得价值超过 1000 美元的生存必需品。包括四周份的应急食品、净水设备等等，全部免费。

speaker: Host
text_en: But with everything going on right now, there's no telling how long it'll be available. So go to preparewiththejudge.com right now to see what you can get for free. Don't wait until it's too late. Get your complete preparedness set up today. Go to preparewiththejudge.com. That's preparewiththejudge.com.
text: 但以现在的局势来看，谁也说不好这个优惠还能持续多久。所以现在就去 preparewiththejudge.com，看看你能免费拿到什么。别等到为时已晚。今天就把你的完整应急准备方案配齐。去 preparewiththejudge.com。就是 preparewiththejudge.com。

speaker: Host
text_en: Professor Mearsheimer, welcome here. Before we get to a general conversation about the neocons and the dynamite, dynamite clip from the debate that you and Professor Walt had with Mrs. Newland and Secretary Pompeo, did the United States just resume attacking Iranian military assets, in this case, speedboats.
text: Mearsheimer 教授，欢迎来到这里。在我们开始泛泛聊 neocons，以及你和 Walt 教授同 Newland 女士、Pompeo 国务卿辩论时那段非常劲爆的片段之前，美国是不是刚刚又恢复了对伊朗军事资产的攻击，这次是快艇？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Yes, they did. It's really hard to figure out what's going on here. If you think about it, when we got together last Tuesday, the 19th, I thought we were going to talk about the war restarting, the air campaign beginning again,
text: 对，他们确实这么做了。现在这里到底发生了什么，真的很难判断。你想想看，上周二，也就是 19 号，我们一起聊的时候，我原以为我们要谈的是战争重新开始，空袭行动再次启动，

speaker: Guest
text_en: because that's what Trump had promised on the 18th, last Monday. But instead, he said. On the 19th, when we got together, that peace negotiations had started again and he had been convinced not to start the bombing campaign. Then on Friday,
text: 因为那本来是 Trump 在18号、也就是上周一承诺过的事。但结果他说，到了19号我们碰头的时候，和平谈判又重新开始了，而且有人说服了他，不要启动轰炸行动。然后到了周五，

speaker: Guest
text_en: he announced that he wasn't going to his son's wedding and it looked like the bombing campaign was going to start over this past weekend, over Memorial Day weekend. But then on Saturday and Sunday, word came out that they were close to an agreement.
text: 他宣布自己不去参加儿子的婚礼了，看起来轰炸行动会在刚过去的那个周末、也就是 Memorial Day 周末重新开始。但到了周六和周日，又传出消息说他们已经接近达成协议了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And you started saying to yourself, oh, here we go. We're going to get an agreement now. And then yesterday, Monday, we're bombing targets in Iran again. It's hard to make sense of where we're going on this roller coaster ride.
text: 然后你就开始心想，哦，又来了。现在我们总算要达成协议了。结果到了昨天，也就是周一，我们又开始轰炸 Iran 的目标了。这种过山车一样的局面，真的很难看清我们到底要往哪儿走。

speaker: Host
text_en: The Iranian negotiators are apparently in Doha. trying to work out some kind of a deal there and within hours of their arrival is when the U.S. attacked these speedboats supposedly it said for self-defense. Is there a ceasefire or isn't there? Are there negotiations or aren't there? This is truly unorthodox. In fact, calling it unorthodox is being charitable. It's crazy.
text: Iran 的谈判代表显然人在 Doha，正试图在那里敲定某种协议。而他们抵达后没几个小时，美国就袭击了那些快艇，据说理由是出于自卫。到底有没有停火？到底有没有谈判？这真的太不按常理出牌了。说它不按常理出牌，甚至都算客气了。这简直太疯狂了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: The whole just talk about the negotiating process. First of all, it appears that on our side, we have one person and one person only who's involved in this process. And that person is President Trump,
text: 先说整个谈判过程。首先，看起来我们这边参与这个过程的只有一个人，而且只有这一个人。这个人就是 President Trump，

speaker: Guest
text_en: who constantly is talking about this aspect of the deal or that aspect of the deal. And he's sort of making things up as he goes along. He's changing his mind from day to day. This is just no way to conduct a negotiation.
text: 他不停地谈协议的这个方面、那个方面，基本上就是边走边编，想到哪说到哪。他的想法一天一变。谈判根本不是这么进行的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: This is something that should be done in private. There should be professional teams on both sides instead of on just one side. And they ought to try to work out the differences so that we can shut this war down.
text: 这种事本来就应该私下进行。双方都应该有专业团队，而不是只有一方有。然后他们应该设法把分歧谈拢，这样我们才能让这场战争停下来。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But if you listen to a lot of what President Trump says and then what the Iranians follow up with, it's quite clear that many of the points that President Trump is making, many of the points he's making are.
text: 但如果你去听 President Trump 说的很多话，再看看 Iran 方面随后作出的回应，就会很清楚地发现，President Trump 提出的很多点，他提的很多点其实都是。

speaker: Guest
text_en: are at odds with what is the Iranian position, and there is no way they would accept Trump's various demands. It's a rather bizarre process, and it's hard to see how it leads to a meaningful deal.
text: 这和伊朗的立场是相冲突的，他们根本不可能接受 Trump 提出的各种要求。整个过程相当诡异，很难看出这怎么能达成一项有实质意义的协议。

speaker: Host
text_en: Well, also over the weekend was a massive attack on governmental buildings in Kiev. An arrest was used, although according to Alistair, the arrest was not used on those buildings so that it wouldn't kill civilians. It was used on other assets on the outskirts of Kiev. But all of this taken together is Putin taking the gloves off. What is the significance of an attack of this magnitude?
text: 另外，周末期间 Kiev 的政府大楼还遭到了大规模袭击。期间用了“榛树”导弹，不过按照 Alistair 的说法，这种导弹并没有用来打那些大楼，以免造成平民死亡，而是被用于打击 Kiev 郊外的其他目标。把这些事放在一起看，说明 Putin 已经不再收着打了。这种规模的袭击意味着什么？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Well, it's quite significant because it does appear he's taking the gloves off, and the Russians have made it clear that the United States and other countries should get their diplomats out of Kiev because that was just the first of what will be a string of attacks on Kiev.
text: 这意义相当重大，因为看起来他确实是不再收着打了，而且俄罗斯人已经明确表示，美国和其他国家应该把他们的外交人员撤出 Kiev，因为这只是接下来一连串针对 Kiev 袭击的第一波。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It looks like he's going to really pound the infrastructure in Kiev in serious ways that... have not been done before. And I think this does reflect the fact he's taking the gloves off.
text: 看起来他会以非常严厉的方式重击 Kiev 的基础设施，力度之大是以前从未有过的。我认为这确实反映出他已经不再收着打了。

speaker: Host
text_en: And do you expect a European response? I don't mean verbally, I mean militarily.
text: 那你预计欧洲会作出回应吗？我不是指口头上的，我是指军事上的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: No, I think what the Europeans will do is they will try to increase the number and the quality of the drones that they give to Ukraine and go to considerable lengths to facilitate more Ukrainian attacks on Russia with those drones. I mean, I think what has in good part prompted this attack by the Russians on Kiev and the threat of more attacks on Kiev.
text: 不会。我认为欧洲人会做的是，尽量增加他们提供给乌克兰的无人机数量和质量，并且会想方设法促成乌克兰用这些无人机对俄罗斯发动更多袭击。我的意思是，我认为很大程度上正是这一点促使俄罗斯对 Kiev 发动了这次袭击，也发出了还会继续袭击 Kiev 的威胁。

speaker: Guest
text_en: is the fact that the Ukrainians have been launching more and more drone attacks on the Russians, and the Russians are reaching a point where they believe they've just had enough and it's time to retaliate and do everything they can to put an end to this.
text: 原因就在于，乌克兰人对俄罗斯发动的无人机袭击越来越多，而俄罗斯人已经到了一个他们觉得忍无可忍的时候，认为该进行报复了，并且要尽一切努力结束这种局面。

speaker: Host
text_en: So is the possession of nuclear weapons a deterrent? Because it doesn't seem to scare the Ukrainians at all.
text: 所以，拥有核武器到底算不算一种威慑？因为看起来这对乌克兰人根本没有任何震慑作用。

speaker: Guest
text_en: No, it doesn't. I mean, the fact is that for a long time now, the Ukrainians have felt free to attack the Russian homeland with missiles and with drones. And you want to remember that in August of 2024, Ukraine actually invaded Mother Russia. This is... Quite remarkable. And that invasion was done with help from the British and from the Americans.
text: 不，不是这样。我的意思是，事实是，很长一段时间以来，乌克兰人一直觉得自己可以自由地用导弹和无人机攻击俄罗斯本土。你还要记得，到了 2024 年 8 月，乌克兰实际上已经入侵了俄罗斯母土。这……相当惊人。而且那次入侵是在 British 和 Americans 的帮助下完成的。

speaker: Host
text_en: Is that when they took that little landlocked? province Kalingrad is that what you're talking about or you this know was technically, he's talking about Kurtz this was the curse offensive. right and it took the Russians a while to get them out
text: 你说的是他们拿下那个内陆的小省份 Kalingrad 的时候吗？你是在说那个吗，还是说——严格来说，他说的是 Kurtz，这次是 Kursk offensive，对吧？而且俄罗斯人花了点时间才把他们赶出去。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It did. It took them a good couple months to push them out, which they eventually did. But the mere fact that Ukraine invaded Russian territory is quite remarkable. And the fact that they did it with assistance from the British and the Americans is even more remarkable. remarkable. As I've said on numerous occasions, we would have never thought about doing this during the Cold War.
text: 没错。俄罗斯人确实花了足足几个月才把他们赶出去，最后还是做到了。但乌克兰居然入侵了俄罗斯领土，这件事本身就相当惊人。而且他们还是在 British 和 Americans 的协助下这么做的，那就更惊人了。正如我很多次说过的，在冷战时期，我们根本想都不敢想会这么干。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And then furthermore, the Ukrainians actually attacked again with British and American help one leg of the strategic nuclear triad of the Russian inventory. They went after the bomber leg of the triad. And this was again unthinkable during the Cold War. And it just went to show
text: 而且更进一步说，乌克兰人实际上还在 British 和 Americans 的帮助下，再次攻击了俄罗斯武库中战略核三位一体的一条腿。他们打的是三位一体中的轰炸机这一环。这在冷战时期同样是不可想象的。这也恰恰说明了——

speaker: Guest
text_en: That the Russian nuclear deterrent wasn't really deterring Ukraine from attacking Russian assets on Russian soil to include invading Russia. And there's been tremendous pressure on Trump, not on Trump, on Putin to do something about this for a long time now. But Putin has been very reluctant to up the ante, but it appears that he's now decided that something has to be done.
text: 俄罗斯的核威慑并没有真正阻止乌克兰攻击俄罗斯境内的俄罗斯资产，甚至包括入侵俄罗斯。很长一段时间以来，外界一直在向 Putin 施加巨大压力，要求他对此采取行动——不是对 Trump，是对 Putin。但 Putin 一直非常不愿意升级局势，不过现在看来，他已经决定必须得做点什么了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Done. And I think the bombing of Kiev is the first step in that direction.
text: 必须采取行动。而我认为，对 Kiev 的轰炸就是朝这个方向迈出的第一步。

speaker: Host
text_en: Well, this is asking you to predict what might have happened, but what might have happened if Iran did have a nuclear weapon? Would the United States and Israel have attacked it as boldly and viciously as they did in June and February?
text: 好，这等于是在请你预测一种可能发生的情况，但如果 Iran 当时真的拥有核武器，会怎么样？美国和 Israel 还会像他们在 6 月和 2 月那样，大胆而凶狠地发动攻击吗？

speaker: Guest
text_en: I think the answer is categorically no. And I think all you have to do is look at North Korea to realize just how important it is to have a nuclear weapon when you're dealing with a country like the United States or a country like Israel that might attack you.
text: 我认为答案是绝对不会。我觉得你只要看看 North Korea，就会明白：当你面对像 United States 或 Israel 这样可能攻击你的国家时，拥有核武器到底有多重要。

speaker: Guest
text_en: The United States would not attack North Korea for fear that North Korea would launch its nuclear weapons at South Korea. South Korea and Japan and American military forces that are stationed in those two countries. So from North Korea's point of view, they have the ultimate deterrent. Iran,
text: 美国不会攻击朝鲜，因为担心朝鲜会把核武器打向韩国、日本，以及驻扎在这两个国家的美军。所以从朝鲜的角度来看，他们拥有终极威慑。至于 Iran，

speaker: Guest
text_en: of course, does not. And before February 28th, I don't think many people thought that Iran could shut the Strait down or would shut the Strait down. And that would be a formidable deterrent in itself. But now we know that even though Iran doesn't have nuclear weapons,
text: 当然并没有这种能力。而且在 2 月 28 日之前，我觉得没多少人认为 Iran 能封锁这条海峡，或者会去封锁这条海峡。而这本身就会构成一种强大的威慑。但现在我们知道了，即便 Iran 没有核武器，

speaker: Guest
text_en: it can shut down the Strait. And one could argue that moving forward, the Iranians will have less of an incentive to get nuclear weapons than they otherwise would have because they can shut the Strait down. But nevertheless, if I were an Iranian policymaker, I'd eat grass if that was necessary to acquire a nuclear weapon, because once you have a nuclear weapon. weapon.
text: 它也能封锁这条海峡。你甚至可以说，往后看，Iran 人获取核武器的动机会比原本更弱一些，因为他们可以封锁海峡。但即便如此，如果我是 Iran 的决策者，只要有必要，哪怕吃草我也要搞到核武器，因为一旦你拥有了核武器。武器。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It is extremely unlikely that the Israelis or the Americans will attack you again for fear of ending up in a nuclear war.
text: Israel 或美国就极不可能再次攻击你，因为他们会担心最终演变成一场核战争。

speaker: Host
text_en: But the Ukrainians don't fear Putin using a nuclear weapon.
text: 但乌克兰人并不担心 Putin 会使用核武器。

speaker: Guest
text_en: They don't, which is, I think, quite remarkable. And I think even the Europeans has We all know there's lots of talk inside of Russia these days about attacking targets in Europe, in other words, attacking NATO states that are aiding the Ukrainian offensive against Russia.
text: 他们确实不担心，这一点我觉得相当值得注意。而且我觉得连欧洲人也是这样。我们都知道，这些天 Russia 国内有很多声音在谈论打击欧洲境内的目标，换句话说，就是打击那些在援助乌克兰对俄攻势的 NATO 国家。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Again, because the Russians are fed up and because the Europeans are supporting the Ukrainians, there are powerful incentives for the Russians to attack European states. And these are European states. European states that are in NATO and therefore have an Article 5 guarantee, which means the United States could be dragged into the war.
text: 再说一遍，因为 Russia 已经受够了，而且欧洲人在支持乌克兰人，所以 Russia 有很强的动机去攻击欧洲国家。而这些是欧洲国家，是加入了 NATO 的欧洲国家，因此受到第五条款保障，这意味着美国可能会被拖入这场战争。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Now, most of the rhetoric that you hear coming out of Russia talks about using conventional weapons to attack into Europe, but a number of commentators inside of Russia, and these are prominent commentators, say that if the conventional weapons don't do the trick, Russia should think about using small numbers.
text: 现在，你从 Russia 那边听到的大多数说法，讲的都是用常规武器打击欧洲，但 Russia 国内也有一些评论人士——而且都是很有影响力的评论人士——说，如果常规武器达不到效果，Russia 应该考虑使用少量的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: of nuclear weapons to send a clear message to the West that we live in a nuclear era or in the nuclear era and that Russia is armed to the teeth with nuclear weapons and it will use those weapons if it thinks its survival's at stake.
text: 用核武器来向西方发出一个明确信号：我们生活在一个核时代，或者说就在这个核时代里，而俄罗斯拥有大量核武器，一旦它认为自身生存受到威胁，就会动用这些武器。

speaker: Host
text_en: Does the West fear Russia? Does Western Europe fear Russia?
text: 西方害怕俄罗斯吗？西欧害怕俄罗斯吗？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Well, there's no question the Western elites or the European elites fear Russia. Whether the public does is another matter. I think the elites in Europe, especially in Western Europe, have convinced themselves that the Russians are coming and that something has to be done to create a deterrent to prevent that. In Eastern Europe, there's always been a fear.
text: 嗯，毫无疑问，西方精英或者说欧洲精英是害怕俄罗斯的。至于普通民众怕不怕，那是另一回事。我认为欧洲的精英，尤其是西欧的精英，已经说服自己相信“俄罗斯人要来了”，必须采取点什么措施来建立威慑，防止这种情况发生。在东欧，这种恐惧一直都存在。

speaker: Guest
text_en: and a fear of the Russians. So there's no need to create a boogeyman there. The boogeyman has been there from the beginning. But now in Western Europe, there is this sense that the Russians are coming and something has to be done to contain the Russians. I think this is a fallacious argument. I think the Russians are not even interested in conquering all of Ukraine,
text: 而且一直都有对俄罗斯人的恐惧。所以在那里根本不需要再塑造什么“妖魔化形象”，那个“妖怪”从一开始就在那儿了。但现在在西欧，也出现了一种“俄罗斯人要来了”的感觉，觉得必须采取措施遏制俄罗斯。我认为这是一种站不住脚的论点。我觉得俄罗斯甚至都没兴趣征服整个乌克兰，

speaker: Guest
text_en: much less Eastern Europe. And the idea that they're going to conquer any territory. territory in Western Europe is laughable. I don't know what... These elites are thinking about when they look at how much difficulty the Russians have had conquering the Western, excuse me, the Eastern one-fifth of Ukraine over the past four plus years.
text: 更别说东欧了。至于说他们还要去征服西欧的任何领土，这种想法简直可笑。我真不知道这些精英在想什么——看看过去四年多里，俄罗斯在拿下乌克兰西部，抱歉，是东部五分之一地区这件事上都如此吃力。

speaker: Guest
text_en: This doesn't look like the Wehrmacht. This doesn't look like an army that's poised to overrun Eastern Europe, much less all of Europe. And I think it's not even going to conquer all of Ukraine. I think this is.
text: 这看起来根本不像 Wehrmacht，也不像一支已经准备好横扫东欧、更别说整个欧洲的军队。而且我认为它甚至都不可能征服整个乌克兰。我觉得这件事——

speaker: Guest
text_en: There's virtually no chance of that happening. But nevertheless, the European elites have convinced themselves that they're facing a truly serious threat from Russia, not altogether unlike the Soviet threat during the Cold War,
text: 几乎根本没有发生的可能性。但即便如此，欧洲精英还是说服了自己，认为他们正面临来自俄罗斯的一个极其严重的威胁，某种程度上很像冷战时期的苏联威胁，

speaker: Guest
text_en: and they're now going to set out to deal with this threat. But the big problem they face is the Americans are pulling out of Europe in certain ways, and this means that NATO is no longer the formidable instrument it was.
text: 他们现在也准备着手应对这个威胁。但他们面临的一个大问题是，美国正在以某些方式从欧洲抽身，这意味着 NATO 已经不再是过去那个强大的工具了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It was during the Cold War. And furthermore, the manufacturing capabilities of these European countries are such that they don't have the ability to produce large numbers of weapons to contain the Russians.
text: 那还是在冷战时期。而且，这些欧洲国家的制造能力就是那样，它们根本没有能力大规模生产武器来遏制俄罗斯人。

speaker: Host
text_en: Let's talk about the neocons with whom you had an encounter in. in Canada recently when you and Professor Walt debated former Secretary of State and CIA Director Mike Pompeo and the cookie lady.
text: 我们来说说 neocons。你最近在加拿大和他们有过一次交锋，当时你和 Walt 教授与前国务卿兼 CIA 局长 Mike Pompeo，还有那位“饼干女士”进行了辩论。

speaker: Host
text_en: I don't want to demean her, but I think very little of her, my fellow Princetonian, Victoria Newland, cookie lady, because she supposedly passed out cookies during one of these coups in Maidan. I don't know if it's true or not. What is their thing? What is their argument? What is the core of their belief which is inconsistent with reality?
text: 我不想贬低她，但我确实很看不上她。我那位同为 Princeton 校友的 Victoria Newland，也就是“饼干女士”，因为据说她在 Maidan 的某次政变期间发过饼干。我不知道这事是真是假。他们那一套到底是什么？他们的论点是什么？他们那种与现实不符的核心信念到底是什么？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Well, their basic worldview is that the United States should be prepared to go around the world and slay monsters. Now, you say to yourself, what do they mean by monsters? There's no clear definition of what a monster is. A monster is a country that has interests that are at odds with the United States, that might be a non-democracy that they don't like.
text: 嗯，他们最基本的世界观是，美国应该准备好在全世界到处去斩妖除魔。那你会问，他们说的“怪物”到底是什么意思？其实并没有一个清晰的定义。所谓“怪物”，就是那些利益与美国相冲突的国家，也可能是他们不喜欢的某个非民主国家。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And they believe that American military force provides a magic instrument for transforming these monster states into friendly states. And they believe that if the United States... is smart, what it will do is it will try to do social engineering all across the globe. It will try to change regimes that are not friendly to the United States and the regimes that are friendly to the United States.
text: 而且他们相信，美国的军事力量是一种神奇工具，可以把这些“怪物国家”改造成对美国友好的国家。他们还相信，如果美国足够聪明，它就应该在全球各地搞社会工程，去改变那些对美国不友好的政权，也塑造那些对美国友好的政权。

speaker: Guest
text_en: And of course, once you end up making the world a place that's filled with countries that look like the United States, we will end up living happily ever after. And this explains in good good part why you know people like Pompeo and Newland are deeply committed to a interventionist foreign policy and they're deeply committed to attacking countries like Iran, Iraq,
text: 当然，一旦世界变成一个到处都是看起来像美国那样国家的地方，我们就都能从此幸福快乐地生活下去。这在很大程度上也解释了，为什么像 Pompeo 和 Newland 这样的人会如此坚定地支持干预主义外交政策，也如此坚定地支持攻击 Iran、Iraq、

speaker: Guest
text_en: Cuba and so forth and so on and the argument that Steve and I made of course was that the costs of this strategy is just enormous. this especially when you look at the number of people who end up getting killed and furthermore when you look at the consequences we fail almost every time i mean
text: Cuba 等等。而 Steve 和我当时提出的论点当然是，这种战略的代价实在太巨大了。尤其是当你看到最终有多少人因此丧命时，更不用说它带来的后果了——我们几乎每一次都失败。我的意思是——

speaker: Host
text_en: Do they acknowledge that failure? Do they acknowledge that Trump's disastrous war in Iran has been a failure, even from Trump's perspective?
text: 他们承认这种失败吗？他们承认 Trump 在 Iran 那场灾难性的战争已经失败了吗，哪怕是从 Trump 自己的角度来看？

speaker: Guest
text_en: well they don't directly acknowledge that they in other words when i when i point out How many failures there have been and how few successes, hardly any successes, they don't say you are correct.
text: 嗯，他们并不会直接承认这一点。换句话说，当我指出失败有这么多、成功却这么少，几乎没什么成功时，他们不会说“你说得对”。

speaker: Guest
text_en: They deal with the issue in an oblique way and they, you know, say in very loose terms that we did good here and there and we have the potential to do much more good. And of course, with regard to the Iran war, they're not going to admit.
text: 他们会用一种很拐弯抹角的方式来回应这个问题。你知道，他们会很笼统地说，我们这里那里做得不错，而且我们还有潜力做得更好。当然，至于 Iran 战争，他们是不可能承认的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: admit that this is a catastrophic failure at this point, and they're going to play on the fact that there's some hope that this will work out to our advantage. That's the kind of rhetoric you get.
text: 他们不会承认，到这个时候这已经是一场灾难性的失败了；他们会抓住还有一点希望、事情也许最终会朝对我们有利的方向发展的说法来做文章。你听到的就是这种话术。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But for people who operate inside the bubble, it's almost impossible to face up to the fact that our foreign policy has just been one failure after another. It's really quite remarkable when you sort of think about Afghanistan, Libya. Iraq, Iran,
text: 但对那些活在这个泡泡里的人来说，要正视这样一个事实几乎是不可能的：我们的外交政策就是一次接一次地失败。你仔细想想，Afghanistan、Libya、Iraq、Iran，真的很惊人。

speaker: Guest
text_en: go back to the Bush doctrine. You remember the Bush doctrine in 2003, which was formulated before we went into Iraq. We were supposed to not only knock off the regime in Iraq and democratize Iraq,
text: 再往前说到 Bush doctrine。你还记得 2003 年的 Bush doctrine 吧，那是在我们进入 Iraq 之前制定的。按当时的说法，我们不仅要推翻 Iraq 的政权，把 Iraq 民主化，

speaker: Guest
text_en: but what we were going to do is democratize the entire Middle East. We were going to turn it into a sea of democracies. And of course, that would lead to a pacified Middle East. Because once you create democracies in all those countries, we live happily ever after. It was that kind of logic. But of course, Iraq was a disaster. The Bush doctrine disappeared, although it then got resurrected.
text: 而且我们还要把整个中东都民主化。我们要把那里变成一片民主国家的海洋。当然，那样就会带来一个被安抚下来的中东。因为一旦那些国家都成了民主国家，我们就能从此幸福快乐地生活下去。逻辑就是这么一套。但当然，Iraq 是一场灾难。Bush doctrine 也消失了，不过后来又被翻出来了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: resurrected when we did the attack on Iran, because that was supposed to happen as part of the Bush doctrine back in 2003, but it didn't happen then, but it happened this year. And look at where we are. One of the great disasters in American foreign policy, I argue it'll be seen in time as the greatest foreign policy disaster the United States has ever engaged. pitched in.
text: 在我们攻击 Iran 的时候，它又被翻出来了，因为这本来就是 2003 年 Bush doctrine 的一部分，只是当时没发生，今年却发生了。看看我们现在到了什么地步。这是美国外交政策中的一场重大灾难。我认为，随着时间推移，它会被视为美国有史以来最严重的外交政策灾难。

speaker: Host
text_en: Here's a clip. There are many, many fabulous clips from this debate. Unfortunately, they're proprietary, but this is one that the debate organizers released and so we're able to air it. Chris, cut number four.
text: 这里有一段片段。这场辩论里有很多很多特别精彩的片段。可惜它们是专有内容，不过这是辩论主办方放出来的一段，所以我们可以播。Chris，放第四段。

speaker: Guest
text_en: This is what human decency demands. You don't let a radical regime close off the global economy by firing Shahid missiles into nations that did nothing, right? They're firing into civilians in the United... outed Arum Everett's even just this week. That's just not something that the world can accept. And so this monster is worthy of You the hunt.
text: 这是基本的人类良知所要求的。你不能放任一个激进政权朝那些什么都没做的国家发射 Shahid 导弹，从而切断全球经济，对吧？他们在向平民开火，就在 United... 甚至就在这周还曝光了 Arum Everett's。这种事世界根本不可能接受。所以这个怪物值得被追捕。

speaker: Host
text_en: do know, Mike, that we started the war.
text: 你知道的，Mike，这场战争是我们先挑起来的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: No, absolutely not. Absolutely not. Oh no, no, no, no. They weren't firing those missiles
text: 不，绝对不是。绝对不是。哦不，不不不不。他们当时并没有发射那些导弹。

speaker: Host
text_en: Welcome. until Israel and the United States attacked Iran. Welcome. You do understand Welcome that, right?
text: 欢迎。直到 Israel 和 United States 攻击 Iran 之后，他们才开始发射。欢迎。你明白这一点吧？

speaker: Guest
text_en: to 1979 when the revolutionary regime began to kill Europeans.
text: 要追溯到 1979 年，当时这个革命政权开始杀害欧洲人。

speaker: Host
text_en: You do know that we opened... He overthrew the democratically elected government
text: 你知道是我们先动的手……他推翻了那个民选政府。

speaker: Guest
text_en: in nineteen fifty four.

speaker: Host
text_en: yeah, but just just for the record you talk you were talking about You this you you don't think Iran has you think Iran's forgotten about that?
text: 对，但先说明一下，你刚才说的是这个——你不觉得 Iran 还记得那件事吗？

speaker: Guest
text_en: No, I suspect they
text: 不，我怀疑他们

speaker: Host
text_en: You
text: 你

speaker: Guest
text_en: have not.
text: 并没有忘。

speaker: Host
text_en: don't think Iran remembers that we supported Saddam Hussein in the Iran Iraq war?
text: 你不觉得 Iran 还记得我们在 Iran-Iraq war 期间支持过 Saddam Hussein 吗？

speaker: Guest
text_en: I I
text: 我，我

speaker: Host
text_en: You know
text: 你知道

speaker: Guest
text_en: think they I have forgotten know this.
text: 觉得他们——我是说——当然知道这件事。

speaker: Host
text_en: about this?
text: 知道这件事吗？

speaker: Guest
text_en: I I know this. Other countries have history.
text: 我，我知道这个。其他国家也有自己的历史。

speaker: Host
text_en: Do you think Iran is a monster? Okay. There
text: 你觉得 Iran 是个怪物吗？好吧，这就来了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: you go.
text: 你看吧。

speaker: Host
text_en: I there you don't go.
text: 我看你这就不对了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: think they're I don't think they're desire I'm glad I don't If I don't
text: 我觉得他们……我不觉得他们是……我很高兴我不……如果我不……

speaker: Host
text_en: Here live we go.
text: 好，开始了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: there, I think many countries do many bad things, including my own, and I wouldn't call the United States a monster.
text: 我是说，我觉得很多国家都做过很多坏事，包括我自己的国家，但我不会把 United States 称作怪物。

speaker: Host
text_en: But Stephen Do notes, you also
text: 但是 Stephen Do 指出，你也

speaker: Guest
text_en: Stephen's no.
text: Stephen 不是。

speaker: Host
text_en: do you also remember that we wouldn't have had October 7th if we hadn't had Iranian support for Hamas?
text: 你也记得吧，如果没有 Iran 对 Hamas 的支持，就不会有 10 月 7 日那次袭击？

speaker: Guest
text_en: I agree, I don't we wouldn't have had October 7th if we'd gotten peace in the Middle East, and that's a much longer story.
text: 我同意。我觉得如果中东实现了和平，也就不会有 10 月 7 日，那就是另一个更长的故事了。

speaker: Host
text_en: It is and every single U.S. President has tried and will continue to.
text: 是，而且每一位美国总统都试过，也还会继续试。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Not very hard.
text: 也没多努力。

speaker: Host
text_en: But it is very important to emphasize there's no evidence that Iran even knew about October 7th, much less caused
text: 但很重要的一点是要强调，没有证据表明 Iran 事先知道 10 月 7 日的事，更别说是他们一手造成的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Oh my goodness.
text: 我的天哪。

speaker: Host
text_en: it. And so my goodness, All right.
text: 这件事。所以，天哪，好吧。

speaker: Guest
text_en: I'm sure it is lovely
text: 我相信那地方肯定很不错。

speaker: Host
text_en: So there to is simply,
text: 所以那边其实就是，

speaker: Guest
text_en: live where you live and
text: 住在你住的地方，然后

speaker: Host
text_en: there's
text: 根本

speaker: Guest
text_en: believe that.
text: 就信这个。

speaker: Host
text_en: no evidence in the
text: 没有任何证据表明

speaker: Guest
text_en: my public goodness. record of that.
text: 我公开行善。没有这方面的记录。

speaker: Host
text_en: Oh my goodness. Oh my goodness. There is no evidence. And she says, I'm sure it's lovely to live where you live. She's got a problem with Chicago and therefore you don't know what you're talking about. This is crazy. Did they present facts or just emotion against you and Steve?
text: 我的天啊。我的天啊。根本没有任何证据。她还说，我相信你住的地方肯定很不错。她就是对 Chicago 有意见，所以就觉得你根本不知道自己在说什么。这也太离谱了。他们拿出的是事实，还是只是用情绪来反对你和 Steve？

speaker: Guest
text_en: Well, sometimes they presented facts, but many times they didn't. And that was a perfect example of that. The fact is, there is no evidence in the public record that Iran. And knew about what was going to happen on October 7th, much less planned it. There's just no evidence. And you would have expected Mike Pompeo, if he thought I was wrong, to provide the evidence that showed that I was wrong.
text: 嗯，有时候他们会摆出事实，但很多时候并不会。那次就是个再典型不过的例子。事实是，公开记录里没有任何证据表明 Iran 事先知道 10 月 7 日会发生什么，更别说是参与策划了。就是没有证据。如果 Mike Pompeo 觉得我错了，你本来会期待他拿出证据来证明我错了。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But he didn't provide any evidence. He just laughed at me and they pretended that we were two fools, you know, who lived in an ivory tower and didn't know much about the real world. But as you know, that's not.
text: 但他并没有拿出任何证据。他只是嘲笑我，然后他们装得好像我们是两个傻子，你知道的，像是活在象牙塔里、对现实世界一无所知的人。但你也知道，事实并不是这样。

speaker: Guest
text_en: It's not the way you're supposed to argue in these debates. You have a bunch of sophisticated and well-educated people getting together to discuss important issues. And if there's disagreement,
text: 这根本不是这类辩论该有的论证方式。一群老练、受过良好教育的人聚在一起讨论重要议题。如果有分歧，

speaker: Guest
text_en: you want to show that the other side is wrong because that side has the facts wrong. But of course, we did not have the facts wrong. And the facts that Steve was presenting to Mike Pompeo are very powerful set of facts.
text: 你应该证明对方错了，是因为对方把事实搞错了。但当然，我们并没有把事实搞错。而且 Steve 当时给 Mike Pompeo 摆出来的那些事实，分量非常重。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We've had our gun sites. It's on Iran in one way or another since 1953 when we overthrew the Mossadegh government. And you could go on and on. You know, Steve pointed out that we supported Saddam Hussein during the 1980-88 Iran-Iraq war. Not only do we support. Iraq in that war. But we helped Saddam Hussein produce chemical weapons that he used against the Iranians. We could go on and on here.
text: 自从 1953 年我们推翻 Mossadegh 政府以来，我们就一直在以这样或那样的方式把枪口对准 Iran。这类事真是说都说不完。你知道，Steve 提到过，1980 到 1988 年的两伊战争期间，我们支持 Saddam Hussein。我们不只是支持 Iraq 打那场仗，我们还帮助 Saddam Hussein 生产化学武器，而他把这些武器用在了 Iranians 身上。这些例子真是可以一直列下去。

speaker: Guest
text_en: The idea that the Iranians are monsters who are constantly attacking innocent Israel and innocent America is just not a serious argument.
text: 说什么 Iranians 是怪物，老是在攻击无辜的 Israel 和无辜的 America，这种说法根本算不上严肃的论点。

speaker: Host
text_en: Did Mrs. Newland mention her husband's famous or infamous article in The Atlantic that the war is a disaster and Trump has no way out and he's not going to achieve any of his goals? No, she didn't. I would not be surprised if she agrees with him.
text: Newland 夫人有没有提到她丈夫在 The Atlantic 上那篇很有名——或者说臭名昭著——的文章？那篇文章说这场战争是一场灾难，Trump 根本没有退路，也不可能实现他的任何目标。没有，她没提。我一点也不会惊讶她其实同意她丈夫的看法。

speaker: Guest
text_en: By the way, she pointed out that she disagreed with Mike Pompeo on a number of issues. And one of the key issues that she pointed to was the JCPOA. As you know, it was shortly after Pompeo became Secretary of State that we pulled out of the JCPOA.
text: 顺便说一句，她提到自己在不少问题上都不同意 Mike Pompeo。其中一个她特别指出的关键分歧，就是 JCPOA。你也知道，就在 Pompeo 出任 Secretary of State 后不久，我们就退出了 JCPOA。

speaker: Guest
text_en: a huge mistake, of course. And Victoria Newland said that she agreed with us that it was a mistake to pull out of the JCPOA. She said she thought the JCPOA had flaws, but those flaws could have been fixed.
text: 当然，这是个巨大的错误。Victoria Newland 说，她同意我们的看法，认为退出 JCPOA 是个错误。她说她觉得 JCPOA 确实有缺陷，但这些缺陷本来是可以修补的。

speaker: Guest
text_en: We should have used it as a basis for creating a better deal. But it was a mistake, she said, and I think quite correctly, to pull out of the JCPOA. And I would not be surprised if we were to pull out of the JCPOA.
text: 我们本来应该以它为基础，去达成一个更好的协议。但她说，退出 JCPOA 是个错误，我觉得她这话说得相当对。而且如果我们真的退出 JCPOA，我也不会感到意外。

speaker: Guest
text_en: be surprised if she agrees with her husband on the argument, which of course Steve and I made, that the Iran war is a disaster. But I think we had no reason to link her to her husband. I mean, they're two separate people who have separate opinions.
text: 如果她同意她丈夫的观点——当然，Steve 和我也提出过这个观点——认为伊朗战争是一场灾难，我也不会感到意外。但我觉得我们没有理由把她和她丈夫绑在一起。我是说，他们是两个独立的人，各自有各自的看法。

speaker: Host
text_en: right, right. If people want to watch the full debate at Professor Mere Sharma, how can they do so?
text: 对，对。如果大家想看完整辩论，Professor Mere Sharma，要怎么做？

speaker: Guest
text_en: I think all you have to do is type in month debate and go to their website. And I believe you have to pay $25 to watch the full debate.
text: 我想你只要输入 Munk debate，然后去他们的网站就行了。我记得看完整场辩论要付 25 美元。

speaker: Host
text_en: It's M-U-N-K, Monk.
text: 是 M-U-N-K，Munk。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Yes, M-U-N-K. It was actually a quite fascinating debate. Whenever you engage in debates like this, you're always hindered somewhat by the format. You know, the other side makes claims that you don't get an opportunity to not. down.
text: 对，M-U-N-K。其实那场辩论相当精彩。每次参加这种辩论，都会多少受到形式限制。你知道，对方会提出一些说法，而你并不总有机会把它们一一驳倒。

speaker: Guest
text_en: But this, of course, applies to both sides. So I think to some extent, both sides in any debate of this sort come away a bit frustrated. But we went on for a long time. It's a very lengthy debate and there was plenty of opportunities for us to get our arguments out, as you could see in the clip where Steve was sparring with Mike Pompeo. There was a lot of that throughout the entire debate. Bye.
text: 不过这当然对双方都一样。所以我觉得，在这种类型的辩论里，双方某种程度上都会带着一点挫败感离场。但我们那场谈了很久，辩论时间非常长，我们也有很多机会把自己的论点讲出来。就像你在那段片段里看到的，Steve 和 Mike Pompeo 有来有回地交锋。整场辩论里这种场面很多。再见。

speaker: Host
text_en: I know Mrs. Newland is no fan of our fellow Columbia faculty member, our mutual friend, Professor Jeffrey Sachs, but Sachs has said that no person has done more damage to Ukraine in the modern era than Victoria Newland.
text: 我知道 Nuland 女士并不喜欢我们在 Columbia 的同事、也是我们共同的朋友 Jeffrey Sachs 教授，但 Sachs 说过，在现代以来，没有谁对 Ukraine 造成的伤害比 Victoria Nuland 更大。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Well, Victoria Nuland was an arch proponent of NATO expansion into Ukraine. She wanted to bring Ukraine into the alliance, and she also played a key role in toppling the regime in February of 2014. We don't know all the details at this point, but the details we do know shows that she was a key player. And I think that there's no question that the decision to bring Ukraine into NATO.
text: 嗯，Victoria Nuland 一直是推动 NATO 向 Ukraine 扩张的强硬支持者。她想把 Ukraine 拉进这个联盟，而且她在 2014 年 2 月推翻当时政权这件事上也扮演了关键角色。到目前为止，我们还不知道全部细节，但我们已经知道的情况表明，她是其中的关键人物。我认为，决定把 Ukraine 拉进 NATO 这件事，毫无疑问

speaker: Guest
text_en: was a remarkably foolish decision and our participation in the coup in February 2014 which of course Victoria Newland was involved in was another catastrophic mistake and set us on the road to the war that we're now in.
text: 是一个极其愚蠢的决定，而我们参与了 2014 年 2 月的那场政变——当然，Victoria Nuland 也牵涉其中——这是另一个灾难性的错误，并把我们一步步带上了如今这场战争的道路。

speaker: Host
text_en: Professor Mare-Schrammer, thank you very much for your time. Always a pleasure, my dear friend. We'll look forward to seeing you next week.
text: Mare-Schrammer 教授，非常感谢您抽出时间。老朋友，和您聊天总是很愉快。期待下周再见到您。

speaker: Guest
text_en: Likewise, Judge.
text: 彼此彼此，法官。

speaker: Host
text_en: Thank you. All the best. Coming up at 10 o'clock, if you're watching us live today in 32 minutes, Ambassador Chaz Freeman right after him at 1030, Larry Johnson at one this afternoon, Scott Ritter at two this afternoon, Matt Ho at three this afternoon. Colonel Karen Kwiatkowski, Judge Napolitano for Judging Freedom.
text: 谢谢，祝您一切顺利。接下来是 10 点钟的节目，如果你今天在看我们的直播，那就是 32 分钟后。之后 10 点半是 Ambassador Chas Freeman，再之后是今天下午 1 点 Larry Johnson，下午 2 点 Scott Ritter，下午 3 点 Matt Ho。Karen Kwiatkowski 上校，我是 Judge Napolitano，这里是 Judging Freedom。
